retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkalemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia is defined as a serum or plasma potassium level above the upper limits of normal, usually greater than 5.0 mEq/L to 5.5 mEq/L. While mild hyperkalemia is usually asymptomatic, high potassium levels may cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, or paralysis. Symptoms usually develop at higher levels, 6.5 mEq/L to 7 mEq/L, but the rate of change is more important than the numerical value. Patients with chronic hyperkalemia may be asymptomatic at increased levels, while patients with dramatic, acute potassium shifts may develop severe symptoms at lower ones. Infants have higher baseline levels than children and adults. This activity reviews the causes, pathophysiology, and presentation of hyperkalemia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dehydration -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Serum potassium levels may be abnormal in patients with dehydration, most frequently hypokalemia due to diarrheal stool losses. With significant bicarbonate losses from stool, metabolic acidosis develops and can progress to poor tissue perfusion and ketosis. When hypovolemia and metabolic acidosis co-occur, the serum potassium rises as potassium shifts from the intracellular to the extracellular space. Monitoring serum potassium levels is essential to guide therapy and avoid complications of hyperkalemia, such as cardiac dysrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkalemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia is defined as a serum or plasma potassium level above the upper limits of normal, usually greater than 5.0 mEq/L to 5.5 mEq/L. While mild hyperkalemia is usually asymptomatic, high potassium levels may cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, or paralysis. Symptoms usually develop at higher levels, 6.5 mEq/L to 7 mEq/L, but the rate of change is more important than the numerical value. Patients with chronic hyperkalemia may be asymptomatic at increased levels, while patients with dramatic, acute potassium shifts may develop severe symptoms at lower ones. Infants have higher baseline levels than children and adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypokalemic Metabolic Alkalosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis is described in the history and physical and evaluation and summarized in the diagram below: Bartter syndrome Congenital chloride-losing diarrhea Glucocorticoid therapy and Cushing syndrome Hyperaldosteronism Hypercalciuria Hypomagnesemia Milk-alkali syndrome Pediatric hypercalcemia Pediatric hypokalemia Pediatric hyponatremia Posthypercapnic alkalosis Sinonasal manifestations of cystic fibrosis Uric acid stones [Diagram 4]

retrieved_docs.title: Alkalosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Bartter syndrome: This autosomal recessive condition is primarily caused by mutations in the NKCC2 and ROMK transporters in the thick ascending loop of Henle. [9] Excessive distal delivery of sodium results in enhanced distal convoluted tubule sodium reabsorption and exchange with the positively charged potassium or hydrogen ion, which leads to increased loss of potassium in urine and increased hydrogen secretion. Bicarbonate is increased secondary to decreased hydrogen ion secretion due to hyperaldosteronism. In addition, loss of the electropositivity of the thick ascending loop of Henle results in the inability to reabsorb magnesium and calcium through claudin channels, leading to nephrocalcinosis. About 50% of cases present in the first year of life and can present as failure to thrive, polyuria, polydipsia, and vomiting. [10] There are 5 known variants. Please see StatPearls' companion reference, "Bartter Syndrome ," for more details.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperaldosteronism -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Liddle syndrome: This is a rare genetic disorder that typically presents in childhood with low aldosterone levels. This condition manifests as hypertension, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis, resembling mineralocorticoid excess disorders. Liddle syndrome is often referred to as pseudohyperaldosteronism and is characterized by increased urinary potassium excretion and sodium reabsorption in the renal collecting tubules despite low aldosterone levels. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Liddle Syndrome (Pseudohyperaldosteronism) ," for additional information.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkalemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudohyperkalemia is quite common and represents a false elevation in measured potassium due to specimen collection, handling, or other causes. Hyperkalemia should always be confirmed before aggressive treatment in cases where the serum potassium is elevated without explanation. True hyperkalemia may be caused by increased potassium intake, transcellular movement of intracellular potassium into the extracellular space, and decreased renal excretion. The urgency of therapy depends on symptoms, serum levels, and causes of hyperkalemia. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Diabetic Ketoacidosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximate potassium deficits in children with DKA are 3 to 6 mEq/kg. However, serum potassium levels are usually normal or slightly elevated at presentation due to the shift of potassium ions from the intracellular to extracellular space. Osmotic diuresis, elevated aldosterone concentrations in response to intravascular volume depletion, and ketoacid excretion may also result in urinary potassium loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkalemia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia is unusual in the general population, reported in less than 5% of the population worldwide, but may affect up to 10% of all hospitalized patients. Most cases in hospitalized patients are due to medications and renal insufficiency. Diabetes, malignancy, extremes of age, and acidosis are other important causes in inpatients. Hyperkalemia is rare in children but may occur in up to 50% of premature infants. Hyperkalemia is more commonly reported in men than women, perhaps due to increased muscle mass and higher rates of rhabdomyolysis, and increased prevalence of the neuromuscular disease. Other factors include non-Black patients and older age. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Fluid Management -- Technique or Treatment -- Tonicity
retrieved_docs.content: In 2018, the American Academy of Pediatrics published a key action statement that states, “children between twenty-eight days to eighteen years of age requiring maintenance intravascular fluids should receive isotonic solutions with appropriate potassium chloride (KCl) and dextrose.” This can significantly reduce the risk of hyponatremia.” [5] Based on this key action statement, the standard of care is for potassium chloride to be added to maintenance fluids unless hyperkalemia is present or renal function is compromised. Also, if the duration of IV fluids will be short, fluids without potassium can be given. The guidelines for potassium vary greatly, and some recommended 10 mEq/L for children less than 10 kg, but the majority recommend 20 mEq/L of KCl regardless of weight. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkalemia -- Etiology -- Impaired Potassium Excretion
retrieved_docs.content: Acute or chronic kidney disease is a common cause of hyperkalemia. Hyperkalemia is usually not seen until the glomerular filtration rate falls below 30 ml/min. This is commonly due to primary renal dysfunction but may be due to acute volume depletion from dehydration or bleeding or decreased circulating blood volume due to congestive heart failure or cirrhosis. Tubular dysfunction due to aldosterone deficiency or insensitivity can also cause hyperkalemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Potassium -- Administration -- Pediatric patients
retrieved_docs.content: Oral: Mild-to-moderate hypokalemia can be treated with 1 to 2 mEq/kg/day in divided doses. Dose adjustments should based on clinical response. The typical range is 1 to 5 mEq/kg/day. No more than 2 mEq/kg should be administered per dose, with a maximum of 40 mEq per dose. Serum potassium levels should be checked at least 2 hours after dosing. Repeat doses as necessary based on laboratory results. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiopulmonary Arrest in Children -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypokalemia or hyperkalemia: Both low and high potassium levels may cause cardiac arrest. The underlying conditions causing potassium imbalances must be considered, such as gastrointestinal or renal disorders. Electrocardiogram (ECG) findings of hyperkalemia include peaked T waves, widening of the QRS complex, and sine wave patterns. Hypokalemia manifests as flattened T waves, prominent U waves, and possible QRS widening on ECG.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypokalemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional team approach to managing hypokalemia ensures comprehensive patient care and better outcomes. Primary care physicians, internists, and pediatricians are central in diagnosing and managing hypokalemia, conducting initial evaluations, and coordinating care with specialists as needed. Nephrologists may be involved in caring for individuals with renal hypokalemia etiologies, providing expertise in kidney function assessment and management. Endocrinologists may be consulted for cases involving hormonal imbalances affecting potassium regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dehydration -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolic acidosis and dehydration frequently co-occur, and the pathophysiology is multifactorial. Acidosis can result from excess bicarbonate losses in diarrheal stools or urinary abnormalities resulting from conditions such as renal tubular acidosis and renal failure. Other disease processes causing metabolic acidosis include sepsis, DKA, ketosis of starvation, lactic acidosis due to inadequate tissue perfusion, and ingestion of toxins. Electrolyte abnormalities often accompany dehydration. For example, severe diarrhea with excessive potassium losses may cause hypokalemia, and infants with pyloric stenosis lose sodium and hydrochloric acid in the emesis, resulting in hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Potassium -- Administration -- Pediatric patients
retrieved_docs.content: Intermittent IV Infusion: Severe hypokalemia should be treated with 0.5 to 1 mEq/kg per dose, with a maximum of 40 mEq per infusion. Continuous ECG monitoring should be performed for infusion rates exceeding 0.5 mEq/kg/hour. Serum potassium levels should be evaluated 1 to 2 hours after the infusion is complete, and repeat dosing should be performed if needed based on laboratory results. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2020
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium ion and potassium supplements are directly linked, as potassium supplements are formulations designed to provide potassium ion to the body. By delivering potassium ion, these supplements help correct potassium deficiencies, support normal electrolyte balance, and maintain proper cellular function, making them an important therapeutic option in the management of conditions such as hypokalemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia and hyperosmolality are classified within fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base disorders, the inverse of اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات (electrolyte imbalance) and mapped to ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما (plasma hyperosmolality). These disorders encompass a spectrum of child conditions including فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyperkalemia), متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم (hypokalemia syndrome), زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية (increased anion‑gap metabolic acidosis), فرط حمض يوريك الدم (hyperuricemia), متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم (hyponatremia syndrome), and نقص كلوريد الدم (hypochloremia). They are linked to غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) and intersect with diabetic pathophysiology: the human INSR protein is the molecular target of insulin aspart, which treats مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes) and مرض السكري نوع 2 (type 2 diabetes). Both diabetes types are associated with فشل كلوي حاد (acute kidney failure) and ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما, illustrating how osmotic and renal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 117
retrieved_docs.content: as children. Acidosis commonly co‑occurs with hyperkalemia, highlighting the interdependence of acid‑base status and potassium homeostasis. Alcoholic hepatitis (الْتِهابُ الكَبِدِ الكحوليّ) is clinically associated with the same fluid, electrolyte and acid‑base disturbances, illustrating how hepatic injury can precipitate systemic electrolyte and acid‑base abnormalities. Collectively, these relationships depict an integrated network in which renal failure, plasma osmolality changes, diabetes, dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, acid‑base disorders and hepatic inflammation are interlinked, underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of renal, metabolic and acid‑base disorders in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 117
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia is classified as a child of fluid‑and‑electrolyte disorders (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات), a subgroup of metabolic disorders that also includes electrolyte imbalance, carbohydrate metabolism disorder, hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), hypokalemia syndrome (متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم), increased anion gap, hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم), hyponatremia syndrome (متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم), and hypochloremia, all of which are characterized by increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما). Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with hyperglycemia, type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1), type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2), and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, while hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) is further linked to hyperkalemia. These fluid‑electrolyte disturbances contribute to the serious complication hyperosmolar hyperglycemic coma (غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري), which belongs to Diseases of the Endocrine Pancreas Including Diabetes, a

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with low blood glucose (نقص تركيز السكر), and it frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض). This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with hyperkalemia should be evaluated for possible hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis, as the simultaneous presence of these disturbances can affect diagnosis, treatment decisions, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperosmolality and hypernatriemia are classified as part of the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance, reflecting their role as disturbances in plasma osmolality and sodium concentration that impact overall homeostatic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1820
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات) is a disorder characterized by abnormal concentrations of key ions in the blood, and it encompasses several specific clinical entities. One component of this disorder is potassium deficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم), which reflects low serum potassium levels and can lead to muscle weakness, cardiac arrhythmias, and metabolic disturbances. Another manifestation is an increased anion gap (زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية), indicating the presence of unmeasured anions such as lactate or ketoacids and often signaling metabolic acidosis. Hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم), the elevation of serum uric acid, may also arise within the context of electrolyte disturbances and is associated with gout, kidney stones, and renal impairment. Finally, low blood chloride (نقص كلوريد الدم) represents hypochloremia, a condition that can exacerbate metabolic alkalosis and affect fluid balance. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which electrolyte homeostasis can be disrupted, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and correction of electrolyte abnormalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2686
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney injury, acidosis, hyperkalemia, and extrarenal blood nitrogen are closely interrelated. Acute kidney injury is clinically associated with both acidosis and hyperkalemia, indicating that patients with sudden loss of renal function frequently develop metabolic acidosis and elevated serum potassium levels. In addition, acidosis and hyperkalemia commonly co‑occur, reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms in renal failure. Acute kidney injury also includes the condition termed extrarenal blood nitrogen, a manifestation of nitrogenous waste accumulation outside the kidneys. Recognizing these linked abnormalities is essential for timely diagnosis and comprehensive management of patients with acute kidney injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol intoxication (تَشَمُّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is clinically associated with hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), indicating that patients who present with severe alcohol‑related toxicity are at risk for elevated serum potassium levels. In addition, hyperkalemia frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض), reflecting the common metabolic disturbance in which excess potassium and a lowered blood pH are observed together. Recognizing these interconnections is important for clinicians, as the presence of alcohol intoxication should prompt evaluation for hyperkalemia, and the detection of hyperkalemia should lead to assessment for accompanying acidosis to guide appropriate treatment and monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance is a broader disorder that includes both hyperuricemia and hypochloremia as specific manifestations, indicating that disturbances in electrolyte homeostasis can present as elevated uric acid levels in the blood as well as reduced chloride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2598
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia is classified under fluid and electrolyte disorders, which themselves are a subset of the broader category اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات (electrolyte imbalance). Fluid and electrolyte disorders are linked to غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري (diabetic hyperosmolar coma), indicating that disturbances in fluid and electrolyte balance can precipitate or accompany this severe hyperglycemic emergency. Within the umbrella of اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات, several specific conditions are identified as child entities: فرط حمض يوريك الدم (hyperuricemia), متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم (hyponatremia syndrome), and نقص كلوريد الدم (hypochloremia). Clinically, التهاب الحويضة والكلية (pyelonephritis) is associated with متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم, suggesting that kidney infection can contribute to or exacerbate hyponatremia. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians recognize how acute hypernatremia, other electrolyte disturbances, and related complications such as diabetic hyperosmolar coma and pyelonephritis are interrelated, guiding comprehensive assessment and management of patients with fluid‑electrolyte disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1584
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders constitute a broad category that includes اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات (electrolyte imbalance). Within اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات, specific disturbances such as زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية (increased anion gap), فرط حمض يوريك الدم (hyperuricemia), and متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم (syndrome of hyponatremia) are recognized as child conditions. Fluid and electrolyte disorders are also linked to غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري (diabetic hyperosmolar coma), reflecting the impact of severe osmotic shifts on fluid and electrolyte homeostasis. Additionally, التهاب الحويضة والكلية (pyelonephritis) is clinically associated with متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم, indicating that renal infection can contribute to the development of hyponatremia. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected nature of fluid‑electrolyte disturbances, metabolic complications, and infectious processes in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: clinical domain linked to the parent category electrolyte imbalance (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات). Acute hypernatremia, hyperosmolality, increased plasma osmolality and hyperosmolar diabetic coma are mapped here, while the electrolyte‑imbalance hierarchy includes hyperkalemia (associated with hypoglycemia), potassium‑deficiency syndrome, increased anion‑gap, hyperuricemia, and hypochloremia (linked to acute pancreatitis). Endocrine, metabolic and nutritional disorders are bidirectionally connected with electrolyte imbalance and carbohydrate‑metabolism disorders and extend to developmental conditions such as sexual immaturity and other sexual disorders. Cystic fibrosis (including phenotypes without meconium ileus) is situated within fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base disorders, highlighting its propensity for complex metabolic disturbances. Acute kidney failure (acute renal failure, فشل كلوي حاد) is tightly associated with type 2 diabetes (including insulin‑requiring forms), increased plasma

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2606
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) is categorized under other endocrine disorders, indicating that it is considered a component of the broader group of endocrine system diseases. Clinically, hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is associated with hypoglycemia, suggesting that patients who experience low blood‑sugar levels may also develop elevated serum potassium. In addition, hyperkalemia frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض), reflecting a common electrolyte and acid‑base disturbance that can accompany high potassium levels. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of evaluating electrolyte balance and acid‑base status when managing endocrine disorders such as hypoglycemia, as concurrent hyperkalemia and acidosis may influence diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2855
retrieved_docs.content: فقر الدم (anemia) is clinically associated with متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم (blood potassium deficiency syndrome). This relationship indicates that individuals presenting with anemia may also exhibit hypokalemia, and clinicians should be attentive to the possibility of concurrent potassium deficiency when diagnosing and treating anemia, as the coexistence can affect therapeutic choices and overall patient management.
